attrs.alt: Azure RBAC authorization flow: deny assignments checked first, override all Allow grants — implicit deny if no matching allow

content.text: Deny assignments block specific operations for specific principals at a scope, regardless of what role assignments might grant. They implement the principle that an explicit deny always overrides an explicit allow — no role assignment, including Owner, can override a deny assignment.

Unlike role assignments, deny assignments cannot be created by regular administrators. They are created by specific Azure systems: Azure Blueprints (when a blueprint uses resource locking), Azure Policy with the `denyAction` effect, and Azure Managed Applications. This restriction is intentional — a broadly assignable deny mechanism would create a powerful tool for privilege escalation or intentional lockout.

# Deny assignments block operations regardless of any role assignment
# They override all Allow grants from any role

# You CANNOT create deny assignments directly via PowerShell or CLI
# They are created by:
#   1. Azure Blueprints (when deploying a locked blueprint)
#   2. Azure Policy denyAction effect (managed deny)
#   3. Azure Managed Applications (resource locks on managed resources)

# You CAN read/list deny assignments
Get-AzDenyAssignment -Scope '/subscriptions/SUB-ID'

# List deny assignments at and above a resource group
Get-AzDenyAssignment -ResourceGroupName 'rg-protected'

# Get details of a specific deny assignment
Get-AzDenyAssignment -Id '/subscriptions/SUB/providers/Microsoft.Authorization/denyAssignments/DENY-ID'

# Check if a deny assignment is blocking a specific principal
Get-AzDenyAssignment | Where-Object {
    $_.Principals.Id -contains 'USER-OBJECT-ID'
}
Specific actions from blueprint lock mode
Any user with write on locks
Any user with write on locks

When a Blueprint artifact is deployed with a lock mode of `DoNotDelete` or `ReadOnly`, Azure Blueprints creates a deny assignment at the artifact scope. The deny assignment blocks all users (via an all-principals token) except the Blueprint service principal itself, which must retain access to manage blueprint lifecycle. This is the most commonly encountered deny assignment in production environments.

# Azure Blueprints: most common source of deny assignments
# When a blueprint artifact has Lock Mode = DoNotDelete or ReadOnly,
# a deny assignment is created at the artifact's scope.

# DoNotDelete lock: blocks delete operations but allows reads and writes
# ReadOnly lock: blocks all write and delete operations (strongest lock)

# The deny assignment created by Blueprints:
# - Name: 'Deny assignment created by blueprint ...'
# - Principals: [All principals] (represented as a special all-principals token)
# - ExcludePrincipals: [Blueprint service principal] (so the blueprint can manage)
# - Actions: varies by lock mode
# - Scope: the deployed resource or resource group

# View blueprint deny assignments
Get-AzDenyAssignment | Where-Object {
    $_.DenyAssignmentName -like '*blueprint*'
} | Select-Object DenyAssignmentName, Scope, @{N='Principals';E={$_.Principals.Id -join ', '}}

# To remove: delete the blueprint assignment that created the deny
# (cannot remove the deny directly)
Remove-AzBlueprintAssignment -Name 'my-blueprint-assignment' -SubscriptionId 'SUB-ID'

To remove a Blueprint-created deny assignment, delete the Blueprint assignment that created it — the deny assignment cannot be removed directly. Removing the Blueprint assignment also removes all resources it deployed (depending on configuration).

The `denyAction` policy effect is the modern approach to creating deny assignments programmatically. When a policy with `denyAction` is assigned, the policy engine evaluates resources and automatically creates deny assignments on matching resources. This lets platform teams protect any resource satisfying a policy condition (e.g. tagged `critical=true`) without Blueprints.

Resource locks are a simpler, manually-applied mechanism that blocks deletion (`CanNotDelete`) or all writes and deletions (`ReadOnly`) on a resource group or resource. Like deny assignments, resource locks apply to everyone including Owner. Unlike deny assignments, resource locks can be created by any user with write permission on the lock resource type.

# Azure Resource Locks — a simpler but related mechanism
# Resource locks are NOT deny assignments but achieve similar protection

# CanNotDelete lock: allow read/write, block delete
New-AzResourceLock \`
    -LockName 'prod-no-delete' \`
    -LockLevel CanNotDelete \`
    -ResourceGroupName 'rg-production' \`
    -Force

# ReadOnly lock: block all write and delete operations
New-AzResourceLock \`
    -LockName 'prod-readonly' \`
    -LockLevel ReadOnly \`
    -ResourceGroupName 'rg-production' \`
    -Force

# Resource locks apply to all users including Owner and admins
# Even subscription Owner cannot delete a resource with a lock
# (must remove the lock first, which itself requires write on the lock)

# Difference from deny assignments:
# Resource locks: created by users with write on locks, simple two-level model
# Deny assignments: created by system/Blueprints, more granular (specific Actions)

# List all locks in a subscription
Get-AzResourceLock -Scope '/subscriptions/SUB-ID'

# Remove a lock (requires write permission on the lock itself)
Remove-AzResourceLock -LockName 'prod-no-delete' -ResourceGroupName 'rg-production' -Force

When a principal receives `AuthorizationFailed` despite having an appropriate role, the first check should be deny assignments and resource locks, not the role assignment itself. The authorization flow evaluates denies before allows — a deny anywhere in the scope hierarchy blocks access regardless of role grants.
Deny Assignments from Azure Managed Applications

Azure Managed Applications deploy a managed resource group that is controlled by the publisher (not the customer). The platform automatically creates deny assignments blocking customers from modifying the managed resources. This enforces the publisher's SLA and integrity guarantees. Unlike Blueprint denies, customers cannot remove Managed Application deny assignments — the publisher controls the lifecycle.

# Azure Managed Applications also create deny assignments
# When a Managed Application is deployed, it creates a managed resource group
# The publisher (service provider) gets a deny assignment blocking
# the customer from modifying resources in the managed RG

# View managed application deny assignments
Get-AzDenyAssignment | Where-Object {
    $_.DenyAssignmentName -like '*managedApp*' -or
    $_.Scope -match 'mrg-'  # managed resource groups often have 'mrg-' prefix
} | Select-Object DenyAssignmentName, Scope

# List managed applications in a subscription
Get-AzManagedApplication | Select-Object Name, ResourceGroupName, ManagedResourceGroupId

# If a deny is blocking a needed operation in a managed RG:
# Contact the publisher — they control the managed RG's resources
# Alternative: negotiate 'just enough access' to specific sub-resources
# with the publisher's support portal or contract

Deny assignments inherit to child scopes by default, the same way role assignments do. A deny assignment placed at the subscription level blocks the denied operations on all resource groups and individual resources within that subscription. The `DoNotApplyToChildScopes` property can restrict a deny to exactly one scope with no inheritance.
Protect entire subscription or RG hierarchy
Deny applies only at the declared scope
Block one specific RG without affecting resources inside
Auditing Deny Assignments Across the Enterprise

Deny assignments from multiple sources (Blueprints, Policy, Managed Apps) can accumulate across subscriptions. Regular cross-subscription audits identify all active deny assignments, their source, and which principals are affected or excluded.
Comparing Deny Assignments with Other Blocking Mechanisms

Azure offers four mechanisms that block access: deny assignments (created by system services), resource locks (created by admins), Azure Policy deny effect (blocks non-compliant writes), and Conditional Access policies (blocks authentication). Each operates at a different layer with different bypass options and audit trails.

# Deny assignment vs other blocking mechanisms — comparison

# 1. Deny Assignment (Blueprint/Policy denyAction)
#    - Blocks: specific Actions/DataActions
#    - Who: specific principals (or all)
#    - Can bypass: ExcludePrincipals list only
#    - Remove: delete the Blueprint/Policy assignment that created it
#    - Audit: Get-AzDenyAssignment

# 2. Resource Lock (CanNotDelete / ReadOnly)
#    - Blocks: delete (CND) or write+delete (RO)
#    - Who: EVERYONE including Owner (no exceptions)
#    - Can bypass: remove the lock (needs write on lock)
#    - Remove: Remove-AzResourceLock
#    - Audit: Get-AzResourceLock

# 3. Azure Policy (Deny effect — not denyAction)
#    - Blocks: create/update operations that violate policy
#    - Who: all principals
#    - Can bypass: policy exemption
#    - Remove: delete/modify policy assignment or add exemption
#    - Audit: az policy state list

# 4. Conditional Access (Entra ID)
#    - Blocks: authentication/authorization to Azure management
#    - Who: users matching CA conditions
#    - Can bypass: exclusion in CA policy
#    - Remove: modify CA policy

Deny assignments are the strongest access control primitive in Azure RBAC — they override all role assignments including Owner. Because they cannot be created by regular administrators, encountering a deny assignment almost always means a Blueprint, a Policy denyAction, or a Managed Application created it. Reading and auditing deny assignments is always available; removing them requires addressing the system that created them.
Deny assignments override ALL Allow grants — even Owner cannot bypass an active deny.
Regular administrators cannot create deny assignments directly — only Blueprints, Policy denyAction, and Managed Applications can.
To remove a Blueprint deny: delete the Blueprint assignment.
To remove a Policy deny: delete or modify the policy assignment.
Resource locks are simpler (CanNotDelete / ReadOnly) and can be created by any user with lock write permissions.
When debugging AuthorizationFailed: check deny assignments and resource locks before checking role assignments.
